data.title: Chinese E-Commerce Platforms Could Face Profit Hit From Food-Delivery Competition — Market Talk
data.description: Chinese e-commerce platforms could suffer a profit hit due to competition in the food-delivery space, Goldman Sachs analysts say in a commentary. On a 12-month horizon, GS expects absolute food delivery investment and losses to peak in the quarter ending September, which could create an inflection point for e-commerce stocks sometime over 2H. It adds that the market has largely factored in expectations of sharply weaker profit for JD.com in 2Q, and that new user growth offers some upside. The analysts reiterate their buy rating on PDD Holdings, which they say isn't participating directly in the food-delivery battle. They maintain a buy rating on Alibaba Group despite the company's recent significant investments in on-demand delivery likely weighing on its e-commerce segment. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

data.title: Zhejiang RIFA Precision Machinery Subsidiary Placed Under Receivership Following Non-Payment of Debt
data.description:
A banking consortium has taken over Zhejiang RIFA Precision Machinery subsidiary Airwork and placed it under receivership after defaulting on a syndicated loan with a balance of $88.3 million.

Both Airwork and the consortium failed to appoint a financial adviser for the sale of the subsidiary's assets for the repayment of the loan, leading to the takeover, according to a Friday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

Airwork signed the syndicated loan agreement in 2019 and extended the loan through July 31, 2026, the filing said.

Calibre Partners was named receiver and will implement takeover and bankruptcy management on Airwork, the filing said.

data.description:

data.title: European shares fall as US tariff deadline looms
data.description:
Key points:
  • Italian lender BPER boosts Pop Sondrio bid to $6.39 billion
  • China announces duties up to 34.9% on EU brandy producers
  • Germany's DAX down around 1%, Spain's IBEX down 1.7%

European shares fell nearly 1% on Friday, as investors weighed uncertainty over U.S. trade deals ahead of the July 9 deadline.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index was down 0.9% at 539.05 points, as of 0856 GMT, and on track to log a weekly decline.

Other regional indexes also declined, with Germany's DAX down 0.9%, France's CAC 40 losing 1.2%, Spain's IBEX falling 1.7%, and the UK's FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 down 0.4%.

U.S. President Donald Trump said that Washington will start sending letters to countries on Friday specifying what tariff rates they will face on imports to the United States.

With Trump's 90-day pause on higher U.S. tariffs ending next week, investors have taken a cautious stance as several large trading partners, including the European Union, are yet to clinch trade deals.

The EU is pushing for an "agreement in principle" with the U.S. ahead of the deadline, seeking immediate tariff relief in key sectors as part of any trade deal.

"If we look at the EU, there are tariffs of 25% on autos and auto parts and 50% on aluminum and steel and it seems to be that those tariffs will not be taken down in the negotiations and that would be a traumatically negative outcome for the EU," said Jochen Stanzl, chief market analyst at CMC Markets.

"Everybody's just hoping that those sectoral tariffs are going to go away but you don't know. So, investors are forced to wait and see."

Meanwhile, China's Commerce Ministry issued its final ruling on an investigation into brandy imports from the EU, most of which is cognac from France.

China will impose duties of up to 34.9% for five years, starting July 5, on EU brandy producers that either lack minimum price commitments or violate agreed pricing terms.

Euro zone banks (.SX7E), mining-related stocks (.SXPP), and retailers (.SXRP) were the top laggards, falling 1.4% each.

Trump's tax-cut legislation cleared its final hurdle in the U.S. Congress on Thursday, and he plans to sign it into law later in the day.

Italian lender BPER increased its bid for smaller rival Popolare di Sondrio to 5.44 billion euros ($6.39 billion). Shares of BPER were down 2.3% on Friday.

Swiss engineering company ABB said it had signed a 15-year fleet service contract with Royal Caribbean Group, but its shares fell 1.5% despite the announcement.

Sweden's Loomis fell 3.8% to trade at the bottom of the STOXX 600 index.

Conversely, shares of Rheinmetall rose 1.7% after Deutsche bank raised its target price on the German defence contractor.

Signalling a potential cooling in consumer demand, data showed that Italian retail sales in May dipped 0.4% month-on-month.

data.title: Ethiopia says it agrees $1 billion financing deal with World Bank
data.description:
Ethiopia said on Friday that it had signed a $1 billion financing agreement with the World Bank to support its economic reform programme and boost growth.

Its finance ministry said in a post on Facebook that the financing would take the form of a concessional loan and grant.

The money will bolster government efforts to ensure financial sector stability, enhance trade competitiveness and strengthen domestic resource mobilization, it added.

The World Bank's local office in Addis Ababa did not immediately respond to a request to confirm that the financing had been agreed and say what it was for.

This week the International Monetary Fund's board approved the latest review of the East African country's $3.4 billion loan programme, unlocking access to another $262.3 million tranche.

Reforms backed by the IMF include last year's floating of the country's birr currency and moves to open up the previously-closed economy to the private sector.

The IMF said it was important for Ethiopia to improve foreign exchange market functioning, boost domestic revenues, restore external debt sustainability and enhance fiscal transparency.

data.title: Stendörren Fastigheter Buys SEK70 Million Finnish Warehouse from Wulff Group
data.description:
Stendörren Fastigheter (STEF-B.ST) said Friday it acquired a warehouse property in Espoo, Finland, from Finnish workplace products and services company Wulff Group at an agreed value of 70 million kronor.

The Swedish property company noted the 3,600 square metre property, Kilonkartanontie 3, is fully leased under a 10-year agreement, and the annual net operating income from the asset is estimated at 5.5 million kronor.

data.title: Ayfie and Crayon announce cooperation
data.description:
Ayfie International AS and Crayon AS have signed an agreement where Ayfie's AIsolutions will be offered to Crayon's customers as part of their completeportfolio of IT services.

Crayon's customers will get access to Ayfie's AI software technology, ensuringcomplete data sovereignty while utilizing the world's most powerful AIapplications to structure and use a company's internal databases.

"Ayfie's technological market leadership has impressed us, and we experienceincreased demand in our customer base for AI solutions that are secure,flexibleand easy to implement in both SMB and large organization," says AllanJakobsen,EVP Crayon Nordics.

Crayon is a customer-centric innovation and IT services company with over 4000team members across 45 countries. Crayon optimizes businesses' IT estate tohelpthem innovate with expertise they can trust.

"Our vision is to pioneer a new era of intelligent search solutions, whereRetrieval-Augmented Generation technology seamlessly integrates withenterprisesystems to unlock the full potential of data. Our new partnership with aleadinginternational player such as Crayon is a significant step in this direction,"says Herman Sjøberg, CEO of Ayfie.

Ayfie announced in the 2024 Annual Report published on 28 March 2025 that thestrategic direction in 2025 will emphasize broadening market reach throughstrategic partnerships and sales initiatives. The agreement with Crayon ispartof this strategy.

The global generative AI market is projected to expand significantly, with acompound annual growth rate of approximately 36.3 % through 2030, driven bytheincreasing demand for intelligent, automated solutions that augment businessprocesses.

data.title: Treasury and Dollar Performances Signal Approaching End of U.S. Exceptionalism — Market Talk
data.description: The golden era of U.S. economic and geopolitial leadership--or U.S. exceptionalism--is coming to an end, Pictet Asset Management says. "We are seeing the first signs of this with the breakdown in the relationship between the dollar and U.S. Treasurys," Pictet says. Historically, yields on U.S. Treasurys have moved in the opposite direction to the currency, it says. "Whenever turbulence hits, investors worldwide would gravitate to the perceived safety of U.S. Treasurys and the dollar, sending yields lower and the greenback higher." However, since President Trump's so-called Liberation Day tariff announcements, that link has broken, with yields having risen while the dollar has weakened. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0519 ET - The golden era of U.S. economic and geopolitial leadership--or U.S. exceptionalism--is coming to an end, Pictet Asset Management says. "We are seeing the first signs of this with the breakdown in the relationship between the dollar and U.S. Treasurys," Pictet says. Historically, yields on U.S. Treasurys have moved in the opposite direction to the currency, it says. "Whenever turbulence hits, investors worldwide would gravitate to the perceived safety of U.S. Treasurys and the dollar, sending yields lower and the greenback higher." However, since President Trump's so-called Liberation Day tariff announcements, that link has broken, with yields having risen while the dollar has weakened. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0449 ET - President Trump's major budget package, which was passed by Congress, could push U.S. debt higher, J.P. Morgan Asset Management's Tilmann Galler says in a note. "If the tax cuts remain in place permanently, this could cause U.S. debt to rise to over 130% of GDP," the capital market strategist says. While the program could generate positive growth effects in the short term, it could also lead to significant disruptions in bond markets, he says. "For example, confidence in the U.S. dollar is beginning to dwindle as a consequence." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0420 ET - President Trump's tax and spending bill could have negative implications for the dollar in the long term, Commerzbank's Volkmar Baur says in a note. The House passed the bill Thursday and Trump is expected to sign it into law Friday. The dollar could benefit in the short term as increased fiscal stimulus could support growth, Baur says. "In the long term, however, the bill only adds to our concerns about the status of the dollar." The bill includes cuts to investment incentives for future technologies and the healthcare sector. Higher spending on deportations will further reduce labor supply, limiting economic growth potential. U.S. borrowing will remain high, meaning there is no buffer for any possible crisis. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0416 ET - Gold futures rise and are on track for a weekly gain despite interest rate headwinds and trade uncertainty. Futures are up 0.3% at $3,353.10 a troy ounce. The precious metal is poised to post weekly gains of around 2% despite falling in the prior session on stronger-than-expected U.S. nonfarm payroll data, MUFG analysts say in a note. The surprising data has lowered the odds of near-term Federal Reserve interest rate cuts and pushed up the U.S. dollar and Treasury yields, MUFG says. Higher interest rates for longer typically damp the appeal of non-interest bearing bullion, while a stronger dollar and yields compete for gold's safe-haven characteristics. Still, gold remains well-supported by safe-haven demand amid geopolitical and trade uncertainties, including looming tariff deadlines on July 9, MUFG adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0353 ET - The U.S. will likely impose a 10% tariff on all trading partners by the upcoming tariff deadline, while the timeline for negotiations of reciprocal tariffs could be extended, ANZ economists say in a research note. Given President Trump's ambition to reindustrialize the U.S. economy, higher tariffs on select strategic sectors are likely to remain in place, they say. Final trade deals may not be reached by July 9, so ANZ expects some interim bilateral trade agreements. Among these talks, the U.S.-EU dialogue is particularly crucial, as it represents the largest bilateral trade relationship in the world, they note. If the deadline is not extended or reciprocal tariffs are expanded, downside risks to U.S. economic growth and upside risks to inflation could increase, they add.(sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0328 ET - China may still be a target despite its recent progress in trade negotiations with the U.S., say Capital Economics' economists led by Julian Evans-Pritchard. Given its trade truce with the U.S., China isn't at much risk of being hit with higher duties when the 90-day pause on Liberation Day tariffs ends next week, the economists say in a commentary. However, Trump's trade deals with the U.K. and Vietnam suggest the country may remain an indirect target. "It seems that Trump is keen to crack down on the rerouting of Chinese exports via third countries, which has diminished the effectiveness of U.S. tariffs," they say. But doing so will be much easier said than done, they add. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0255 ET - Bitcoin falls as investors turn cautious ahead of the July 9 deadline when the pause in U.S. reciprocal tariffs ends. Only three trade agreements have been secured so far with Vietnam, China and the U.K., well short of expectations, IG analysts say in a note. This has prompted President Trump to shift tactics with plans to send letters to trading partners detailing new tariffs, they say. Meanwhile, the House approved Trump's tax-and-spending bill Thursday. It will add $3.4 trillion to national debt, raising inflationary concerns just as the economy shows strength, the analysts say. That reduces the prospect of interest rate cuts. Bitcoin falls 0.9% to $109,008 after reaching a five-week high of $110,585 Thursday, LSEG data shows. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0232 ET - The dollar retraces earlier gains driven by Thursday's better-than-expected nonfarm payrolls data as concerns over U.S. trade weigh. The end of a 90-day pause on so-called U.S. reciprocal tariffs is due to end on July 9. President Trump said he would start sending letters to trading partners from Friday, laying out the details of new tariffs. Meanwhile, Friday looks set to be a quiet day due to the July 4 U.S. holiday with the U.S. stocks and bond markets closed. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 96.862. It reached a one-week high of 97.422 Thursday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0203 ET - The U.S.-Vietnam trade deal sends a strong signal that tariffs on other countries won't hover near the universal 10% mark--they will be higher, says Ipek Ozkardeskaya at Swissquote Bank. Trump earlier told reporters that the U.S. would start sending letters to countries detailing tariff rates, which could range from 60% or 70% to 10% and 20%. "We'll probably have 10 or 12 [letters] go out tomorrow, and over the next few days." Trump called the rates a "bargain," adding: "I don't want to stretch it too much. We want to keep it pretty reasonable." For smaller countries, tariffs would pretty much be kept the same, he said. Asked if there would be flexibility on when countries would start paying, he said: "Not really, they'll start to pay on August 1." (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0154 ET - The threat to global growth from U.S. tariffs policy remains high, says Shane Oliver, chief economist at AMP. A 20% average U.S. tariff rate is well down on the 30% that would have applied post "Liberation Day," but it's well up on the 3% average U.S. tariff rate at the start of the year, he says. While share markets haven't been too fussed lately about tariffs, they still pose a significant threat to global growth and U.S. inflation. Any 60% or 70% tariffs will likely just be another negotiating ploy, but may be taken badly by share markets, Oliver adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)

2314 ET - The Singapore dollar consolidates against its U.S. counterpart during the Asian session amid concerns over tariffs. If U.S. President Trump unilaterally reimposes high tariffs on July 9 and/or on August 12, when his two tariff truces expire, foreign-exchange markets could become highly volatile, says CBA's Joseph Capurso in a research report. A "large currency reaction is possible because we consider market participants are complacent as illustrated by low implied volatility," says the head of International and Sustainable Economics. USD/SGD is little changed at 1.2746. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2217 ET - Vietnam's trade deal with the U.S. brightens its outlook, say analysts at BMI, a unit of Fitch Solutions. They reckon Vietnam managed to clinch a deal faster than others because it sought relatively lower tariffs rather than insisting on zero, and offered a major market-opening package. Despite the agreed-upon 20% levy, Vietnam's exports are set to keep growing steadily, BMI says. Vietnam's cost competitiveness will likely keep production from relocating to other economies, and BMI expects foreign direct investment to continue flowing. The certainty the deal provides will assure U.S. investors that Vietnam can still be part of their supply chains, and Chinese firms will still be incentivized to move production to Vietnam. BMI now thinks Vietnam's 2025 GDP growth could top its forecast of 6.4%. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

The 30-year gilt yields climbed to a four-week high of 5.452% on Wednesday as investors worried about the prospects of a new U.K. treasury chief and a shift in fiscal rules. The gilt yields have retreated and last trade at 5.315%, Tradeweb data show. "Policymakers now face harder fiscal choices into the autumn to stabilize long-end rates." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

However, given elevated inflation, super-long yields cannot be seen as excessively high, he says. "Even if the cheapness relative to valuations using forwards is reversed, this would likely be achieved via rate hikes that cause shorter interest rates to rise," he says. The weakness of the super-long sector reflects concerns around domestic politics ahead of a July 20 parliamentary election and stalled tariff negotiations with the U.S. In the near term, the focus will be on the 3% yield level for the 30-year JGB, he says. data.title: Eurozone Producer Prices Fall at Slower Pace
data.description:
Euro Area industrial producer prices fell by 0.6% month-on-month in May 2025, following a sharper 2.2% decline in April and slightly exceeding market expectations of a 0.5% drop.

Energy prices continued to fall, though at a slower pace (-2.1% vs. -7.8% in April), while prices for intermediate goods declined by 0.1%, matching the previous month’s rate.

Capital goods prices were unchanged, compared with a 0.1% increase in April.

In contrast, prices rose for both durable consumer goods (0.3% vs. 0.1%) and non-durable consumer goods (0.2% vs. 0.3%).

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0914 GMT - Antofagasta and Anglo American fall as LME three-month copper prices slide 1% to $9,854.0 a metric ton on holiday-thinned trading. In recent weeks, prices have risen on month on persistent U.S. tariff uncertainties. Antofagasta slips 2.1% to 1,893 pence but remains up 3.6% on week and 34% over the past three months. Anglo American falls 2% to 2,198 pence. It remains up 2.2% on week and 22% over the past three months. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0901 GMT - Italy's UniCredit will be able to focus on its pursuit of Commerzbank as it looks likely to withdraw its bid for domestic peer Banco BPM, AlphaValue says in a research note. UniCredit's CEO wrote to the German Chancellor about the future of Commerzbank. The probability of a bid on Commerzbank is low but is the best case scenario as it could boost UniCredit's value by up to 15%, analyst David Grinsztajn says. A somewhat likely alternative sees it remaining a strategic shareholder with a positive but more modest valuation impact of less than 5%. The analyst also considers a merger of equals between Commerzbank and UniCredit's German subsidiary HVB, which would seem politically correct but suboptimal in terms of capital and quite improbable. A withdrawal is highly probable and would have no valuation impact, he adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0848 GMT - Chesnara's latest deal materially enhances the longer-term sustainability of its dividend growth, Berenberg says in a research note after the insurer bought HSBC's life operations in the U.K. for 260 million pounds. "This is a significant positive for the investment thesis in our view," analyst Michael Huttner writes. The group said that the key attraction of the transaction is that 80% of its 800 million pound total future cash flow expected from the tie-up will be realized after the fifth year, he says. This includes a conservative assumption for cost synergies and the ratio of cash to deal value is over 3 times, which is a very attractive multiple, he writes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0844 GMT - Sterling faces further losses as markets are yet to fully factor in U.K. fiscal risks, Monex Europe analyst say in a note. Sterling fell after Prime Minister Keir Starmer failed to initially back Treasury chief Rachel Reeves in parliament Wednesday. U.K. politics could garner more attention Friday given the lack of market catalysts elsewhere in the world, the analysts say. "Indeed, reports overnight that former Labour leader Jeremy Corbyn is setting up a new political party certainly caught our eye." A challenger to the left of Labour would add pressure on Starmer, they say. Sterling rises 0.1% to $1.3660 but falls against the euro, which is up 0.1% at 0.8614 pounds. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0840 GMT - The latest data shows the London market continues to be sluggish, but the public-equity market might be reaching an inflection point, White & Case Capital Markets analyst Jonathan Parry says in a note. London's nine initial public offerings have raised only 182.8 million pounds in the first half, a fall compared with the 526.7 million pounds raised in eight IPOs in the same period of the prior year, Parry says. Still, the concerted efforts of the London Stock Exchange and the Financial Conduct Authority to reform the regulatory framework are starting to generate momentum, he says. "As a result of these reforms, the London market is now on a level regulatory playing field with other leading listing venues and may be approaching an inflection point that we hope will kickstart a new IPO cycle," he says.(anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0804 GMT - The battle over China's food-delivery market is unlikely to end any time soon, Goldman Sachs analysts say. The intensity of competition between e-commerce players who have entered the space and local services leader Meituan in food delivery and instant shopping has reached new heights, they write in a note. "Unlike previous investments by e-commerce players that were scaled back within a few quarters, we believe the duration of investments this time will extend for longer- given all of the companies are shifting focus to become China's 'everyday app' for transactions across goods and services." Retail players' aim is likely to use food-delivery in cross-selling to their e-commerce offerings, they add.(tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0748 GMT - Samsung Electronics could post a 2Q earnings miss largely due to likely weaker-than-expected semiconductor business, Hana Securities analysts Roko Kim and Y.G. Kim say in a note. They expect the South Korean tech giant's 2Q operating profit to come in at 5.8 trillion won, down 12% from their previous estimate of 6.6 trillion won, after revising down their profit forecast for the memory-chip segment. That would miss the latest FactSet-compiled consensus forecast of 6.5 trillion won. Softer-than-expected shipments of high-bandwidth-memory products likely weighed on the company's DRAM segment, while NAND chip prices may have fallen at a sharper-than-expected pace, they say. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0738 GMT - Thailand's retail sector 2Q same-store sales likely contracted on some headwinds, CGS International's Chaiyatorn Sricharoen says in a research report. Domestic consumption notably deteriorated from April, on factors including falling tourist arrivals and worries over potential U.S. tariff increases, the analyst says. A high base from last year's El Nino-driven sales also weighed on grocery and home improvement segments. However, CGS International maintains the sector's neutral rating as most of the consumption weakness appears priced in. The brokerage favors defensive names with clear earnings visibility like Berli Jucker and CP All. It has add ratings on both and target price of THB26.00 on Berli Jucker and THB65.25 on CP All.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0729 GMT - Aker BP's second-quarter production was slightly light, RBC Capital Markets analysts write. The 415,000 barrels of oil equivalent a day was slightly below the 420,000 barrels the analysts had expected. However, it was in line with the company's full-year production guidance of 390,000 to 420,000 barrels a day, they write. Shares fall 2.5% to 260.9 Norwegian krone. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0723 GMT - European airlines shares fell Friday morning on the second day of a strike of France's air-traffic controllers, which is forcing carriers to cancel hundreds of flights. Air France-KLM is down 0.3%, Wizz Air falls 1.4%, EasyJet dips 0.8%, Ryanair and TUI go down 0.7%, and Deutsche Lufthansa loses 0.4%. In London, International Consolidated Airlines Group--comprising British Airways, Iberia and Vueling among others--is flat. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0720 GMT - Air France-KLM's decision to increase its stake in Swedish flag carrier SAS was largely expected at some point, JPMorgan analyst Harry Gowers says. The deal is relatively small but there are some reasons to be positive about it, he says. Air France-KLM will gain greater access to wealthy Scandinavia, and will be able to benefit from cost synergies as SAS becomes a subsidiary of the group, Gowers says. Financial details are light at this stage, he adds. Shares in Air France-KLM are down 0.4%, dragged by the impact of the second day of France's air-traffic controller strike, which has forced airlines to cancel hundreds of flights. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0710 GMT - Rheinmetall's second-quarter growth will be hindered by January's explosion at its facility in Murcia province, southeast Spain, which affected shipments, Jefferies analysts Chloe Lemarie and Ben Brown say. The German arms maker told a second-quarter pre-close call on Thursday evening that it expects a 200 million-euro headwind from the fire. However, the products are almost ready to be shipped and the impact should be fully recovered by the end of the year, starting in the third quarter, the analysts say. Second-quarter growth was also affected by limited order intake from the German government as it was undergoing its post-election transition, they note. Rheinmetall remains on track to meet its fiscal 2025 guidance, they add. Shares closed at 1,698.50 euros on Thursday.(cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0749 GMT - Oil prices are ticking lower in early trade as investors brace for OPEC+'s meeting this weekend, with expectations for another large output hike already factored in. Brent crude slips 0.2% to $68.68 a barrel, while WTI is flat at $66.99 a barrel. Eight key members of the group are anticipated to approve a fourth production increase of 411,000 barrels a day for August--a move that would increase prospects of a supply glut. Traders are also closely monitoring U.S.-Iran nuclear talks after Axios reported that discussions are set to resume next week in Oslo. Meanwhile, Iran's Foreign Minister, Abbas Araghchi, said the country remains committed to the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty and its safeguards agreement following Tehran's decision to suspend cooperation with the U.N. nuclear watchdog. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: European Midday Briefing: Shares Fall as Trump Tariff Deadline Looms
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Stocks:

European stocks fell Friday as Trump's July 9 tariff deadline loomed ever larger.

If negotiators cannot reach a deal by Wednesday , U.S. tariffs could rise sharply and could prompt tit-for-tat measures.

Amid the risk there may not be agreements, the U.S. could extend negotiations on reciprocal tariffs, ANZ, said.

The U.S.-Vietnam trade deal has sent a strong signal tariffs could be higher than the universal 10% mark, Swissquote Bank said.

Trade and tariffs remained the key uncertainty for eurozone rates markets , according to Societe Generale.

In Germany, May factory orders declined but international demand and orders from outside the eurozone rose, suggesting U.S. firms continued to stockpile goods.

The air traffic control strike in France continued and weighed on aviation shares .

Stocks to Watch

Rheinmetall's second-quarter growth will reflect January's explosion at its facility in southeast Spain, and limited order intake from the German government, according to Jefferies.

U.S. Markets:

Markets are closed today for the country's July 4 holiday after payrolls rose more than expected Thursday.

Forex:

The dollar fell against a basket of currencies after it reached a one-week high Thursday.

The currency could benefit in the short term from Trump's tax and spending bill, but the legislation could have negative implications [https://The dollar could benefit in the short term from President Trump's tax and spending bill, but the legislation could have negative implications for the dollar in the long term, Commerzbank said. https://newsplus.wsj.com/article/realtime/DNCO20250704001310] in the long term, Commerzbank said.

Sterling rose against the dollar but fell against the euro and it faced further losses as markets were yet to factor in the country's fiscal risks, Monex Europe said.

Bonds:

The eurozone bond supply is set to slow down, potentially helping yield spreads to tighten, Societe Generale said, adding this could be supportive for Italian and Spanish bonds.

The 10-year Bund yield was forecast to continue trading in the 2.40%-2.80% range until the end of the year.

The 10-year Treasury yield is expected to stay in the 4.0%-4.5% range in the coming months, Societe Generale said separately.

"While we expect a modest increase in term premia, the rise in long-end yields is likely capped and hence the steepening momentum is likely to wane."

Energy:

Oil prices fell as investors braced for OPEC+'s meeting this weekend and expected another large output hike.

Metals:

Gold futures rose and were on track for a weekly gain of around 2%.

EMEA HEADLINES

Italy's BPER Banca Bumps Popolare Di Sondrio Offer to $6.4 Billion

Italian lender BPER Banca topped up its offer for Banca Popolare di Sondrio by throwing in a cash component to value the smaller rival at around 5.47 billion euros ($6.43 billion).

In February, BPER Banca rushed to join the wave of consolidation sweeping through Italy's crowded banking sector in a bid to gain scale and defend its market position. The offer it put forward for BP Sondrio valued it at roughly 4.3 billion euros based on share prices at the time.

French Regulators Fine Shein for Misleading Discounts

French regulators fined fast-fashion retailer Shein more than $40 million for what they deemed to be misleading practices on prices and environmental claims.

After an 11-month investigation by France's consumer watchdog and its national investigation service, regulators said that Infinite Style E-Commerce, responsible for Shein's products in France, had misled consumers regarding discounts.

Putin Plots Summer of Relentless Attacks on Ukraine

KYIV, Ukraine-As President Trump is pulling back in Ukraine, Russian President Vladimir Putin is pushing forward.

Moscow is ramping up its ground offensives and bombing campaigns against cities across Ukraine as its invasion enters a fourth summer. At the same time, the U.S. decision this week to stop delivery of some weapons to the Ukrainians hands Putin a significant boost to his efforts to weaken Western support that is central to sustaining Kyiv's resistance.

No More Tears. U. K. Finance Minister's Job Appears Safe.

No more tears, a stronger pound, and lower borrowing costs. That is where things stand on Thursday, after the British prime minister made it clear that he supports his finance minister.

On Wednesday, the currency sank and prices of gilts fell, sending yields higher, after Prime Minister Keir Starmer appeared not to back Chancellor of the Exchequer Rachel Reeves during Parliament's weekly debating session, the Prime Minister's Questions. Reeves wiped away tears, televised images showed.

GLOBAL NEWS

Wall Street Worries as Crisis-Level Deficits Become the Government's Default Mode

U.S. budget deficits were already approaching $2 trillion when Republican lawmakers set out to extend and expand tax cuts this year. Interest rates were high and the bond market was jumpy, producing worrying spikes in borrowing costs.

Republicans forged ahead anyway, defying warnings from Wall Street to Washington that they were pushing the country further down a dangerous fiscal path.

China, U.S. Speeding Up Trade Truce, Chinese Commerce Ministry Says

Beijing and Washington are speeding up the implementation of the trade framework reached in London, China's commerce ministry said Friday.

American officials have informed China that they are lifting a series of restrictive measures on Chinese goods, a ministry spokesperson said.

House Passes Trump's Megabill in GOP Triumph

WASHINGTON-The House narrowly passed Republicans' sprawling tax-and-spending bill, delivering a major second-term victory for President Trump and again demonstrating his power to keep GOP lawmakers in line.

The 218-214 vote Thursday sends the measure to Trump's desk, ahead of the July 4 target he set for Congress to finish the legislation that cuts taxes, boosts border security and lowers social safety-net spending. Trump and GOP leaders muscled the bill through the House after an all-night session, despite many lawmakers' misgivings about the version the Senate passed 51-50 earlier this week.

North Korean, in Rare Feat, Flees to Freedom On Foot Through the DMZ

SEOUL-A North Korean man made a daring escape to freedom, traversing on foot one of the world's most fortified places: the Korean demilitarized zone.

He was detected by South Korea's military around 3 a.m. Thursday, according to Seoul's Defense Ministry, navigating through the land-mine-laden DMZ, which stretches more than 2 miles wide and roughly 150 miles long.

The Yellow Sea Is the New Flashpoint for China's Regional Power Play

SEOUL-One day in February, a South Korean research vessel approached a set of unusual structures planted by China in the middle of the Yellow Sea, the narrow body of water that separates the two countries.

Two large Chinese Coast Guard ships and three Chinese boats quickly blocked the South Korean ship's path. The passengers on the small Chinese boats wielded knives to force the Koreans back.
